entries.title: CLI overview
headings: How to think about the CLI
entries.body: CLI overview The You Agent Factory CLI is the operator surface for turning planned work into running workflow state. You use commands to submit work, inspect status, move a task into the next workstation when needed, and run the local quality checks that keep a branch reviewable. How to think about the CLI Think of the CLI as the control plane for the factory rather than a single one-shot generator. A command either creates work, advances work through a workstation, or tells you what happened after a run. The important mental model is that the command you choose determines whether you are creating queue state, inspecting runtime state, or validating the files a workflow produced. Typical commands and outcomes you submit batch --dry-run <path> checks a batch request before it enters the factory, and you submit batch <path> creates durable work items for the runtime to process. you work list and you session list answer different questions: the first shows durable workflow state, while the second shows active or recent runtime sessions. python factory/scripts/setup-workspace.py <prd-name> prepares the worktree for implementation, and repository quality commands such as make check and make test confirm that the generated changes still satisfy the local review gates. What a successful run produces A successful CLI-driven run leaves behind inspectable artifacts instead of hidden automation. Expect to see a named work item, a branch and worktree for implementation, updated docs or code files, append-only progress tracking, and command output that helps an operator tell whether the run advanced, stalled, or needs a manual repair step such as you work move. Where to go next Read the configuration overview next if you need to understand which files and values shape what the CLI will execute. Continue to workflow concepts after that when you want the approval loops, execution stages, and observable run outcomes to make sense as one operating model.

entries.title: Coder / Reviewer pattern
headings: What the review loop looks like
What teams get from the pattern
entries.body: Coder / Reviewer pattern Use this page when you need a practical starting point for understanding the Coder / Reviewer operating pattern inside You Agent Factory. Teams use this pattern when they want one role focused on producing the change and another role focused on validating whether that change should move forward. The split does not guarantee correctness, but it does make ownership clearer and makes review work explicit instead of optional. Who the two roles are Coder The coder is responsible for making the requested change real. In practice that usually means reading the task, inspecting the current system, editing code or docs, running the relevant checks, and showing enough evidence that the change is ready for review. The coder should optimize for correctness and traceability, not only speed. A strong coder handoff leaves the reviewer with concrete outputs to inspect: the diff, test results, and a short explanation of what changed. Reviewer The reviewer is responsible for deciding whether the change is acceptable to merge or whether it needs another pass. That means checking the actual behavior, reading the diff for design fit, verifying tests, and pushing back when the change does not meet the requested standard. The reviewer is not there to re-implement the task. The value of the role is independent judgment about the submitted work: does it solve the stated problem, does it preserve system quality, and is the evidence strong enough to approve? How the handoff works The normal handoff is simple: 1. The coder completes one bounded piece of work. 2. The coder runs the relevant checks and records what was verified. 3. The reviewer inspects the diff and the observable evidence. 4. The reviewer either approves the work or sends it back with concrete changes requested. This handoff keeps coding and judging separate. That separation matters because the person who made the change is often the least reliable person to decide that their own work is fully correct. What the review loop looks like In day-to-day engineering work, the loop is usually short and explicit: 1. The reviewer reads the change against the task, not only against personal style preferences. 2. If something is wrong or unproven, the reviewer sends specific feedback back to the coder. 3. The coder revises the change, reruns the relevant validation, and hands the update back. 4. The loop ends when the reviewer can either approve with confidence or reject because the requested outcome is still not being met. Good review loops are concrete. Feedback should point to the behavior that failed, the risk it creates, and what proof would resolve the concern. What teams get from the pattern Used well, the Coder / Reviewer pattern gives a team a few practical advantages: Better change quality because someone other than the author checks the result. Clearer review ownership because approval is a deliberate step rather than an implied one. More disciplined iteration because rejected work comes back with specific issues to fix. Those outcomes are useful because they improve merge decisions, not because the pattern removes uncertainty. The reviewer still works with incomplete information and has to apply engineering judgment. Where approval gates matter This pattern only works safely when approval is treated as a real gate. The reviewer should be able to stop the change from moving forward when the evidence is weak, the important behavior was not validated, or the implementation does not match the requested outcome. In practice, that usually means the reviewer does not approve until they can trace the request to the diff and to the checks that matter for that change. If the evidence is missing, the correct move is to reject the handoff and ask the coder to come back with stronger validation. Realistic limits and failure modes The most common failure mode is shallow review. A reviewer can read quickly, approve based on trust, and miss the exact behavior that needed scrutiny. Another failure mode is unclear ownership, where the coder assumes the reviewer will catch every issue and the reviewer assumes the coder already validated the risky parts. The reviewer role helps contain those risks by forcing one person to judge the submission independently, but it does not remove the need for engineering judgment. If the review is rushed, the task is ambiguous, or the evidence is weak, the pattern can still fail and should not be treated as automatic safety.

entries.title: Workflow concepts
headings: What you should observe during a run
How the CLI and configuration connect
entries.body: Workflow concepts Workflow concepts explain how the CLI, configuration, approvals, and outputs fit together once you move beyond the first setup path. A workflow run is not just a command invocation: it is a staged operating loop where the factory creates work, advances it through defined checkpoints, and leaves behind evidence that tells you what happened. How workflow execution moves Think of a workflow as a sequence of workstations rather than a single script. A run begins when the CLI submits or advances a named work item, then the factory routes that item through the configured stages for planning, implementation, validation, review, or any other lane your process defines. Each stage has a specific responsibility, so the important first-adoption mental model is that progress happens through explicit transitions, not hidden background magic. Where approvals fit Approvals govern when the system can continue automatically and when it must pause for a human decision. Some stages can run straight through, while others stop at a review or process checkpoint until an operator confirms the result, requests fixes, or rejects the current output. This approval loop is what keeps the factory useful for real engineering work: automation does the repetitive execution, but humans still control the riskier decisions. What you should observe during a run A healthy workflow produces inspectable state at every step. Expect to see durable work-item status, active or completed session records, branch or worktree changes, append-only progress logging, and PR conversation updates when a lane is operating against a review branch. If the run stalls, those same artifacts tell you whether the task is waiting on approval, blocked by a failed quality gate, or ready for the next CLI transition. How the CLI and configuration connect The CLI is how you operate the factory, but configuration is what defines the workflow that the CLI can run. Commands create or advance work, configuration decides which stages exist and where approvals happen, and workflow execution is the observable result of those two surfaces working together. This is why the concepts page matters after the CLI and configuration overviews: it turns those separate ideas into one operating model a new evaluator can reason about. Where to go next Return to the CLI overview if you need the operator command surface, or revisit configuration when you want to inspect which files and settings created a specific workflow path. From there, deeper reference or architecture docs can add more detail without changing this first-adoption workflow model.

entries.title: Configuration
entries.body: Configuration Configuration is the contract between the CLI command you run and the workflow behavior the factory will execute. It tells the system which work items exist, which agents or tools participate, where approvals are required, and which repository files define the process for a given lane. How to think about configuration Think of configuration as the durable operating model for the factory rather than a bag of one-off flags. The CLI starts and advances work, but configuration decides what that work means. A good first-adoption mental model is that commands trigger execution, configuration defines the allowed workflow shape, and progress logs or outputs prove what happened during the run. Main configuration concepts Most teams only need to understand a few concepts at first. The factory is organized around file-driven workflow definitions, task or PRD inputs that name the work to perform, workspace or branch setup that gives each run an isolated place to write files, and approval or review checkpoints that determine when a task continues automatically versus waiting for a human decision. What configuration is responsible for Configuration is responsible for turning a generic workflow engine into your team's operating path. It identifies the workflow templates that can run, the instructions or standards those workflows must follow, the paths where inputs and outputs live, and the command or quality gates that keep a generated branch reviewable before it moves forward. How configuration changes execution Small configuration changes can materially change runtime behavior. Choosing a different workflow definition can add or remove stages, changing approval settings can introduce a pause before review or deployment, and pointing the factory at different task or docs inputs changes which artifacts the CLI produces in a worktree. This is why configuration matters before you think about deeper workflow concepts: it shapes what the operator should expect from the same CLI surface. Where to go next Return to the CLI overview if you need the command surface again, or continue to workflow concepts when you want to connect configuration, approvals, execution stages, and observable outputs into one operated system.

entries.title: Human approval gates
headings: When a workflow should pause for review
What approval gates are meant to catch
What reviewers should inspect before approving
Why approval loops improve safe adoption
How this page fits the rest of the docs
entries.body: Human approval gates Use this page when you need the generated docs shell to point readers to one canonical guide about approval checkpoints in You Agent Factory workflows. This guide sits after workflow concepts because approval gates make the most sense once you understand that the factory advances work through explicit stages with durable outputs and review handoffs. It sits before deeper reviewer-pattern material because approval checkpoints are the simpler concept: they define where a workflow must stop for a human decision before it continues. What this guide is for Treat this page as the entry point for understanding why some workflows should pause instead of running straight through every stage. A reader who lands here should be able to identify approval gates as a deliberate operating-control surface inside the factory rather than a vague safety warning scattered across other docs. When a workflow should pause for review An approval gate should interrupt execution at a meaningful risk boundary, not at every trivial handoff. Use one before the workflow applies a high-impact code change, publishes or deletes something expensive to undo, or turns a low-confidence output into the next irreversible step. The key test is whether the next action could compound a bad assumption faster than the workflow can correct itself. If a stage is about to merge a pull request, edit production configuration, spend significant compute or external-service cost, or fan out a questionable result into more automation, that is a strong signal to pause and ask for bounded human review. What approval gates are meant to catch Approval loops exist to catch avoidable failures while the workflow still has a clean stopping point. They are useful when generated changes are technically plausible but may be out of scope, when the workflow summary does not match the actual diff or artifact, or when the system is acting on inputs that are incomplete, ambiguous, or newly introduced. They are not a sign that every step needs supervision. If a workflow stage is cheap, reversible, and already well validated, adding a human gate usually slows the lane down more than it improves control. Reserve approvals for the moments where a reviewer can materially change the outcome. What reviewers should inspect before approving Treat an approval as an evidence check, not a gut check. Before approving, inspect the artifact the workflow is about to act on: the proposed diff, generated document or output, stage summary, logs, and any validation or test results that explain what changed and whether the result still fits the requested scope. The reviewer should be able to confirm four things from that evidence: the output is correct enough for the stage, the next action is safe, the work still matches the requested scope, and the workflow is not about to take an expensive or hard-to-reverse step on weak evidence. If those signals disagree with each other, or if the summary looks cleaner than the underlying artifact, send the workflow back with a concrete fix request instead of approving on intuition. Why approval loops improve safe adoption Approval loops reduce avoidable failure by stopping the workflow before one wrong assumption compounds into several expensive follow-on actions. They give a reviewer one bounded moment to catch a bad diff, a misleading summary, an unsafe action target, or a low-confidence result before the factory spends more time, money, or trust building on top of it. That control has a cost: every gate slows throughput because the workflow waits for a human decision. Early in adoption, that tradeoff is usually worth it because teams are still learning where the workflow is reliable and where it drifts. Start with more gates at the highest-risk boundaries, then remove or relax the ones that stop finding real issues once validation, logs, and reviewer experience show the workflow can be trusted there. Approval loops improve the odds of safe operation, but they do not guarantee correctness. A weak review can still miss a real problem, and a mature workflow is not automatically safe to run fully autonomously just because earlier runs looked good. How this page fits the rest of the docs Start with workflow concepts if you still need the overall execution model. Continue to the Coder / Reviewer pattern if you want a deeper role-based example of how one approval-oriented handoff works in practice once a workflow reaches a review boundary.

entries.title: Installation
entries.body: Installation Install You Agent Factory locally using the repository's supported Bun and Make workflow before moving into the first workflow run. Prerequisites Start with Bun 1.1 or newer available on your machine because the repository uses Bun for dependency installation, scripts, and test execution. Clone the repository and work from its root so the documented Make targets run against the same command path used by local contributors and CI. Install the repository Run make setup from the repository root to install or refresh dependencies. This target is the supported entrypoint for initial setup and it delegates to bun install instead of requiring a separate manual package-manager path. Validate the install Run make check, make test, and make build after setup to verify that the local install matches pull request validation. These are the authoritative repository-root commands for contributor and PR parity, so the same typecheck, lint, automated tests, and static export checks fail in both places without switching to a CI-only command surface. What success looks like Treat the install as successful when make check, make test, and make build finish without failures from the repository root. If you want the broader local-only foundation sweep before review, run make quality-gate afterward; it intentionally goes beyond PR validation by adding localization, content, accessibility, and static-export contract checks. Continue into quickstart Continue to Quickstart once the repository installs cleanly and the validation path passes so your first workflow run starts from a known-good local setup.

entries.title: Introduction
headings: What You Agent Factory helps you do
Who this setup path is for
entries.body: Introduction You Agent Factory is an open-source, engineering-native platform for turning recurring development work into reusable, inspectable AI agent workflows. What You Agent Factory helps you do Use it when your team has engineering tasks that happen over and over again, such as docs updates, review passes, release checks, or follow-up work after a bug fix, and you want those flows to be repeatable without becoming opaque. Instead of treating each run as a one-off chat, You Agent Factory helps you define a workflow, run it with agents and tools, inspect the outputs, and keep human review in the loop where approval still matters. Who this setup path is for This path is for engineers and technical teams who already work from repositories, files, command-line tools, and review processes, and want a clearer way to structure recurring AI-assisted development work. If you are evaluating whether the project fits your team, this lane gives you the smallest useful sequence: understand the product, install the supported local toolchain, and run one first outcome. Why continue into setup Continue if you want to confirm the local prerequisites and supported command path for this repository before you invest further time. The next pages show the Bun and Make workflow this repo already expects, then close the lane with one first workflow outcome before you move on to deeper guides and concepts. Continue through setup Move next to Installation, then Quickstart, to stay on the first-adoption path.

entries.title: Quickstart
entries.body: Quickstart Run the docs site locally after installation so the first setup path ends in one concrete, reviewer-verifiable outcome. Before you start Complete the Installation step first so the repository dependencies are installed and make check, make test, and make build already pass from the repository root. Start the local docs site Run bun run dev from the repository root. Use the local URL that Bun prints, then open the /docs route inside that running site so you stay in the docs shell instead of relying on homepage copy alone. Verify the first outcome Treat the quickstart as successful when the local docs shell renders the setup sequence and you can move through Introduction, Installation, and Quickstart from the generated sidebar or previous-next navigation. At that point you have confirmed the supported install path, the generated docs navigation, and the local rendering loop that future docs work builds on. Continue into deeper guides Continue into Getting started for the broader docs baseline, then move on to Core concepts when you want the orchestration model behind the site and factory.
